document_summary:
This paper ('The Geometry of Refusal in Large Language Models: Concept Cones and Representational Independence' by Wollschläger et al., 2025) challenges the prevailing assumption that LLM refusal behavior is controlled by a single linear direction in activation space. The authors introduce Refusal Direction Optimization (RDO), a gradient-based representation engineering method that identifies refusal directions more effectively than the prior difference-in-means (DIM) approach, with 40% fewer side effects on TruthfulQA. The key finding is that refusal is governed by multi-dimensional polyhedral cones—geometric structures containing infinitely many directions that all mediate refusal—rather than a single direction.

The paper's second major contribution is the concept of representational independence (RepInd), a stricter criterion than orthogonality for determining whether two directions in activation space are causally independent. The authors show that orthogonal directions can still influence each other through non-linear transformations across layers, and that RepInd correctly identifies truly independent refusal mechanisms. They find at least five representationally independent refusal directions in Gemma 2 2B, and demonstrate that these directions are accessible from input space via GCG adversarial suffixes. Larger models support higher-dimensional refusal cones, linking model scale to the geometric complexity of refusal mechanisms. The framework generalizes beyond refusal to any concept in LLMs by changing optimization targets.

core_concepts.definition: A multi-dimensional polyhedral cone in activation space where all directions within it mediate the same concept (e.g., refusal). Formally: R_N = {Σ λ_i b_i | λ_i ≥ 0} \ {0}, where B = [b_1,...,b_N] is an orthonormal basis. The non-negativity constraint ensures all directions consistently strengthen the concept rather than introducing opposing effects.
relationships: Generalizes the single refusal direction hypothesis from Arditi et al. (2024) to multi-dimensional structures
Basis vectors are found via Refusal Cone Optimization (RCO), an extension of RDO
Cone dimensionality is bounded by model capacity—larger residual stream dimensions support higher-dimensional cones
Directions within cones may exhibit non-linear dependencies despite basis orthogonality, motivating representational independence

core_concepts.definition: Two directions λ, μ are representationally independent under directional ablation if ablating one has no effect on the cosine similarity between the other direction and the model activations, across a set of layers. Stricter than orthogonality because it accounts for non-linear effects propagated through transformer layers.
relationships: Motivated by the empirical finding that orthogonal directions still influence each other in later layers
Builds on Park et al. (2024)'s observation that orthogonality in Euclidean scalar product does not imply causal independence in LLMs
Enforced via an additional loss term (L_RepInd) penalizing changes in cosine similarity under ablation
Applied over first 90% of layers to avoid overly constraining the unembedding layer

core_concepts.definition: A gradient-based algorithm that identifies directions controlling refusal in activation space by optimizing loss functions encoding two refusal properties (Monotonic Scaling and Surgical Ablation) plus a retain loss for minimizing side effects. Unlike DIM, it leverages gradients rather than solely relying on model activations.
relationships: Outperforms difference-in-means (DIM) on activation subtraction attacks and reduces TruthfulQA side effects by 40%
Extended to Refusal Cone Optimization (RCO) for finding multi-dimensional cones
Extended with RepInd loss for finding representationally independent directions
Generalizable to any concept by changing optimization targets

core_concepts.definition: A direction in a model's activation space that, when manipulated (ablated or added), controls whether the model refuses or complies with a request. Must satisfy two properties: Monotonic Scaling (refusal probability scales with coefficient α) and Surgical Ablation (ablating causes compliance on harmful prompts while preserving behavior on harmless ones).
relationships: Prior work (Arditi et al., 2024) assumed a single refusal direction; this paper shows multiple exist
Can be extracted via DIM (prompt-based) or RDO (gradient-based)
Lives within concept cones containing infinitely many such directions
Can be accessed from input space via adversarial suffixes (GCG)

core_concepts.definition: A method for extracting a directional feature from the residual stream by computing the difference in mean activations between datasets of harmful and harmless prompts. The baseline approach from prior work (Arditi et al., 2024; Panickssery et al., 2024).
relationships: Serves as the baseline that RDO improves upon
Used to generate training targets for RDO (ablate DIM to get harmful answers, add DIM to get refusal targets)
For many Qwen 2.5 models, most DIM directions have excessive side effects
Only one viable DIM direction found across all (layer, token) pairs for Qwen 2.5 7B

core_concepts.definition: Projecting the residual stream to a subspace orthogonal to a given direction, effectively removing that direction's influence. Applied across all token positions and all layers. Formally: x̃ = x - r̂r̂ᵀx.
relationships: One of two intervention methods (the other being activation subtraction)
Applied across all layers and token positions (unlike subtraction which targets a single layer)
Used to test Surgical Ablation property of refusal directions
DIM ablation tends to severely impact TruthfulQA; RDO ablation reduces this error by 40%

core_concepts.definition: Subtracting or adding a scaled direction vector from/to the residual stream activations at a specific layer. Subtraction suppresses a concept; addition induces it. Formally: x̌ = x + α·r (addition) or x̌ = x - α·r (subtraction). Applied at a single layer.
relationships: Used to test Monotonic Scaling property
RDO generalizes to subtraction-based attacks despite not being optimized for them
Scaling coefficient α set to norm of DIM direction for fair comparison

core_concepts.definition: A KL-divergence-based loss term ensuring that directional ablation on harmless instructions does not change the model's output. Computed as KL divergence between probability distributions with and without ablation, masked over target response and last chat template token, averaged across tokens. Applied over last 30 tokens.
relationships: Controls the tradeoff between attack success and side effects via weight λ_ret
For Qwen 2.5 3B, λ_ret ≤ 1 gives best generalization; higher values degrade performance
Provides modeling flexibility absent in the DIM approach
Enables RDO to choose target layers freely while limiting side effects

core_concepts.definition: The hypothesis that concepts—from simple to complex—are encoded linearly in a model's residual stream. Supported by the effectiveness of representation engineering methods that extract and manipulate linear directions to control model behavior.
relationships: Foundational assumption for representation engineering (Zou et al., 2023a; Marks & Tegmark, 2024)
This paper extends it: refusal is not just one linear direction but a multi-dimensional cone of linear directions
Park et al. (2024) challenged the assumption that orthogonality implies causal independence, which this paper addresses with RepInd

processes.name: Refusal Direction Optimization (RDO)
steps: Initialize direction r randomly
Sample batch from dataset of (harmful prompts, safe prompts, answer targets, refusal targets, retain targets)
Compute ablation loss: cross-entropy between ablated model output on harmful prompts and answer target
Compute addition loss: cross-entropy between model output with added direction on safe prompts and refusal target
Compute retain loss: KL divergence between ablated and normal model output on safe prompts over retain targets
Combine: L = λ_abl · L_ablation + λ_add · L_addition + λ_ret · L_retain
Update r via gradient descent: r ← r - η∇_r L
Normalize: r ← r / ||r||_2
Repeat until convergence
Select best direction from last 20 training steps using direction selection algorithm
decision_points: Choice of target layer for activation addition (chosen freely, unlike DIM which is layer-specific)
Retain loss weight λ_ret: balances attack success vs side effects (≤1 optimal for Qwen 2.5 3B)
Model-specific vs generic targets: use model-specific because refusal/response styles differ across models
Learning rate reduction schedule: reduce every 5 batches if plateaued, divide by 1/10 up to 2 times
common_mistakes: Using uncensored LLM-generated targets instead of model-specific targets
Setting retain loss weight too high, which degrades attack success significantly
Not selecting from last 20 training steps (using final checkpoint only)

processes.name: Refusal Cone Optimization (RCO)
steps: Initialize orthonormal basis B = [b_1,...,b_N] randomly
Sample batch from dataset
Sample directions uniformly from cone (sample unit vector in positive orthant, project through basis B)
Compute loss on Monte Carlo samples: E[ComputeLoss(r, f, B)] over sampled directions
Compute loss on basis vectors: (1/n) Σ ComputeLoss(b_i, f, B)
Combine: L = L_sample + L_basis
Update B via gradient descent: B ← B - η∇_B L
Project back to orthonormal basis via Gram-Schmidt: B ← GramSchmidt(B)
Repeat until convergence
Select basis where all vectors effectively mediate refusal from last 20 bases; fallback to basis with best sample ASR
decision_points: Cone dimensionality: how many basis vectors to optimize (limited by model capacity—plateaus at 4 for Gemma 2 2B)
Sampling strategy: uniform unit vectors on cone (not convex combinations, which bias toward basis vectors)
Whether to also compute loss on basis vectors (yes—they are cone boundaries and degrade first)
common_mistakes: Sampling convex combinations of basis vectors and normalizing (introduces bias towards basis vectors)
Allowing negative coefficients λ_i (introduces opposing effects, reduces effectiveness)
Pushing dimensionality beyond model capacity (performance degrades significantly)

processes.name: Enforcing Representational Independence
steps: Start with a reference direction (e.g., DIM direction)
Extend RDO Algorithm 1 with RepInd loss term penalizing changes in cosine similarity at last token position when ablating on harmful instructions
Compute RepInd loss over first 90% of layers (avoid constraining unembedding layer)
Optimize new direction to satisfy refusal properties AND be representationally independent of reference
Validate by tracking cosine similarity between new direction and activations under ablation of reference (and vice versa)
Iterate: find additional directions RepInd to DIM AND all previously found RepInd directions
decision_points: RepInd loss weight λ_ind (set to 200, much higher than other loss weights)
Layer cutoff for RepInd loss (0.9 = first 90% of layers)
When to stop iterating (performance degrades after ~5 directions for Gemma 2 2B)
common_mistakes: Applying RepInd loss over all layers including near unembedding (overly constrains output)
Assuming orthogonality implies independence (it does not, due to non-linear cross-layer effects)
Confusing mathematical orthogonality with causal independence in neural networks

processes.name: Target Generation for RDO
steps: Extract DIM refusal direction from model
For harmful answer targets t_answer: ablate DIM direction and generate 30 tokens on harmful prompts
For refusal targets t_refusal: use activation addition of DIM direction on harmless instructions and generate
For retain targets t_retain: generate 29 tokens on harmless instructions without any intervention
Apply retain loss over last 30 tokens (including last token of chat template)
decision_points: Whether to use model-specific targets vs uncensored LLM targets (model-specific preferred)
Number of tokens to generate for each target type (30 for answer/refusal, 29 for retain)
Which attack method to use for initial target generation (DIM ablation, though any effective attack works)
common_mistakes: Generating targets with a different model or uncensored LLM (style mismatch)
Not including the last token of chat template in retain loss computation

processes.name: Evaluation Protocol
steps: Train refusal direction at same layer DIM is extracted from
Set scaling coefficient α to norm of DIM direction for fair comparison
Evaluate jailbreak ASR on JAILBREAKBENCH using STRONGREJECT fine-tuned judge
Test refusal induction via activation addition on 128 harmless ALPACA instructions using substring matching of refusal phrases
Measure side effects via TruthfulQA benchmark performance
Generate model completions with greedy decoding, max 512 tokens
For cones: sample 256 vectors and compute ASR distribution (boxplot)
decision_points: Refusal detection method for addition (substring matching of common refusal phrases)
Number of Monte Carlo samples for cone evaluation (256)
common_mistakes: Not using the same layer and scaling coefficient when comparing DIM and RDO
Ignoring side effects (TruthfulQA) when reporting attack success

expertise_patterns.pattern: Experts optimize directions via gradients rather than relying on statistical summaries (means) of activations
expertise_patterns.novice_mistake: Computing difference-in-means between harmful and harmless prompts and treating the resulting direction as the definitive refusal direction
expertise_patterns.aha_moment: Encoding desired refusal properties directly into a loss function and using gradient descent yields more precise directions with controllable side effects

expertise_patterns.pattern: Experts test for representational independence rather than assuming orthogonality implies causal independence
expertise_patterns.novice_mistake: Enforcing orthogonality between directions and concluding they are independent
expertise_patterns.aha_moment: Non-linear transformations across transformer layers can cause orthogonal directions to influence each other—you must measure actual activation-level effects under intervention

expertise_patterns.pattern: Experts think in terms of geometric regions (cones) rather than single directions when characterizing concepts in activation space
expertise_patterns.novice_mistake: Searching for 'the' refusal direction as if it were unique
expertise_patterns.aha_moment: Refusal is encoded in multi-dimensional cones containing infinitely many valid directions, and larger models support higher-dimensional cones

expertise_patterns.pattern: Experts use model-specific targets for optimization rather than generic or externally-generated ones
expertise_patterns.novice_mistake: Using targets generated by uncensored LLMs or generic refusal/answer templates
expertise_patterns.aha_moment: Language models differ in refusal and response styles—matching targets to the specific model's style yields more effective direction optimization

expertise_patterns.pattern: Experts balance attack success against side effects using explicit retain losses
expertise_patterns.novice_mistake: Maximizing jailbreak ASR without measuring or controlling impact on general capabilities (e.g., TruthfulQA)
expertise_patterns.aha_moment: A retain loss (KL divergence on harmless outputs) provides a tunable knob for trading off attack power vs preservation of model behavior

expertise_patterns.pattern: Experts sample uniformly from cones rather than using convex combinations of basis vectors
expertise_patterns.novice_mistake: Sampling convex combinations of basis vectors and normalizing, which biases samples toward the basis vectors
expertise_patterns.aha_moment: Because directional ablation normalizes r, convex combination + normalization creates non-uniform coverage—instead sample unit vectors in the positive orthant and project through the basis

temporal_evolution.paradigm: Concepts in LLMs are linearly represented; features can be extracted using paired prompts with opposing semantics (e.g., harmful vs harmless). Representation engineering manipulates single directions for concepts like truthfulness, honesty, fairness.
temporal_evolution.change_trigger: Arditi et al. (2024) specifically claimed refusal is mediated by a single direction in activation space

temporal_evolution.paradigm: Refusal in LLMs is controlled by a single direction extractable via difference-in-means (DIM). Jailbreaks rely on manipulating this single direction. Directional ablation of DIM has little benchmark impact except on TruthfulQA.
temporal_evolution.change_trigger: Park et al. (2024) showed orthogonality does not imply causal independence in LLMs; empirical evidence of high side effects for many DIM directions in Qwen 2.5 models

temporal_evolution.paradigm: Refusal is governed by multi-dimensional polyhedral cones (not a single direction). Multiple representationally independent refusal mechanisms exist. Gradient-based optimization (RDO) outperforms prompt-based extraction (DIM) with fewer side effects. Orthogonality ≠ independence; representational independence is the correct criterion.
temporal_evolution.change_trigger: Introduction of gradient-based representation engineering (RDO), concept cones, and representational independence framework

key_metaphors.metaphor: Concept cone as a geometric searchlight: all rays within the cone illuminate the same concept (refusal), but from different angles
key_metaphors.maps_to: A polyhedral cone R_N = {Σ λ_i b_i | λ_i ≥ 0} in activation space where every direction satisfies refusal properties—not just one special direction

key_metaphors.metaphor: Surgical ablation: precisely removing one concept from representations without damaging surrounding behavior, like surgery that removes a tumor without harming healthy tissue
key_metaphors.maps_to: Projecting activations onto the orthogonal complement of a refusal direction, tested by verifying the model answers harmful prompts while preserving harmless behavior

key_metaphors.metaphor: Basis vectors as boundaries of the cone: the extremes that degrade first, like the edges of a structure being most vulnerable to stress
key_metaphors.maps_to: In cone optimization, basis vectors tend to have the lowest ASR because they lie on the cone boundary; computing loss directly on them improves stability

key_metaphors.metaphor: Representational independence as non-interference between control knobs: two truly independent knobs that don't affect each other when turned
key_metaphors.maps_to: Two directions where ablating one does not change the cosine similarity between the other and model activations across layers—unlike orthogonal directions, which can interfere through non-linear transformations

key_metaphors.metaphor: Residual stream as an information highway: a running sum through which information flows and accumulates across transformer layers
key_metaphors.maps_to: The cumulative representation x_i^(l) at each layer, computed as the sum of the initial embedding and all subsequent layer outputs, where directions can be added/removed to steer behavior

key_metaphors.metaphor: Directions as 'accessible from input' vs 'latent properties': distinguishing between live wires the model actually uses vs mathematical artifacts
key_metaphors.maps_to: Testing whether refusal directions can be activated/ablated through input-space perturbations (GCG suffixes) vs only through internal activation manipulation

anti_patterns.name: Single-Direction Fallacy
anti_patterns.description: Assuming that a concept (especially refusal) is controlled by exactly one direction in activation space, then optimizing defenses or attacks around that single direction.
anti_patterns.why_wrong: Refusal spans multi-dimensional cones with infinitely many valid directions. Defending against one direction leaves all other cone directions exploitable. Multiple independent mechanisms drive refusal.
anti_patterns.fix: Search for multi-dimensional concept cones using RCO. Characterize the full geometric structure rather than finding one representative direction.

anti_patterns.name: Orthogonality-as-Independence
anti_patterns.description: Enforcing orthogonality between directions and concluding they are causally independent, i.e., intervening on one won't affect the other.
anti_patterns.why_wrong: Non-linear transformations across transformer layers cause orthogonal directions to influence each other's representation in later layers. Empirically, ablating one orthogonal direction reduces how much the other is represented.
anti_patterns.fix: Use representational independence: directly measure whether ablating one direction changes the cosine similarity between the other and model activations across layers. Add RepInd loss during optimization.

anti_patterns.name: Generic Target Generation
anti_patterns.description: Using targets generated by uncensored LLMs or generic templates when optimizing refusal directions.
anti_patterns.why_wrong: Language models differ in their refusal and response styles. Generic targets create a style mismatch that weakens the optimized direction.
anti_patterns.fix: Generate model-specific targets by using an existing attack (e.g., DIM ablation) on the target model itself to produce answer, refusal, and retain targets in the model's own style.

anti_patterns.name: Ignoring Side Effects
anti_patterns.description: Evaluating refusal direction interventions solely by attack success rate without measuring impact on model capabilities.
anti_patterns.why_wrong: DIM directional ablation severely impacts TruthfulQA performance. For many Qwen 2.5 models, most DIM directions have side effects too high to be viable. An attack that breaks the model's general behavior is not a meaningful jailbreak.
anti_patterns.fix: Include a retain loss (KL divergence on harmless outputs) during optimization and evaluate on benchmarks like TruthfulQA. Tune λ_ret to balance ASR and side effects.

anti_patterns.name: Biased Cone Sampling
anti_patterns.description: Sampling cone directions via convex combinations of basis vectors then normalizing, or using non-uniform sampling strategies.
anti_patterns.why_wrong: Because directional ablation normalizes the direction, convex combination + normalization biases samples toward the basis vectors, giving misleading cone performance estimates.
anti_patterns.fix: Sample unit vectors uniformly from the positive orthant of the cone: sample s ~ Unif(x ∈ R^n_+ : ||x||_2 = 1), then project r = Bs.

anti_patterns.name: Layer-Unrestricted Independence Loss
anti_patterns.description: Applying representational independence loss across all transformer layers including those near the unembedding layer.
anti_patterns.why_wrong: Enforcing RepInd too close to the unembedding layer overly constrains the model's output, degrading both refusal properties and general behavior.
anti_patterns.fix: Apply RepInd loss over only the first 90% of layers (layer cutoff = 0.9).

notable_quotes: Prior work suggests that a single refusal direction in the model's activation space determines whether an LLM refuses a request.
Contrary to prior work, we uncover multiple independent directions and even multi-dimensional concept cones that mediate refusal.
we show that orthogonality alone does not imply independence under intervention, motivating the notion of representational independence that accounts for both linear and non-linear effects.
This suggests that the activation space in language models exhibits a general property where refusal behavior is encoded within multi-dimensional cones rather than a single linear direction.

Larger models appear to support higher-dimensional refusal cones. A plausible explanation is that models with larger residual stream dimensions (e.g., 5120 for the 14B model vs. 1536 for the 1.5B model) allow for more distinct and orthogonal directions that mediate refusal.

Despite enforced orthogonality, ablating RDO indirectly reduces the representation of the DIM direction in the model activations in the later layers, as measured by cosine similarity. This effect is reciprocal, suggesting that orthogonality alone does not guarantee independence throughout the network.
instead of relying solely on orthogonality, we define two directions as representationally independent if ablating one has no effect on how much the other is represented in the model activations.
Despite these strong constraints, we successfully identify at least five such directions that maintain an ASR significantly above random vector intervention.
the higher ASR in the low-sample regime suggests that different directions capture distinct, complementary aspects of the refusal mechanism.

We observe that GCG is able to create suffixes that significantly reduce how much RepInd 1 is represented. These suffixes successfully jailbreak the model 36% of the time, which is similar to the ASR of RepInd 1.
Monotonic Scaling: when using the direction for activation addition/subtraction, the model's probability of refusing instructions should scale monotonically with α.
Surgical Ablation: ablating the refusal direction through projection should cause the model to answer previously refused harmful prompts, while preserving normal behavior on harmless inputs.
Our gradient-based representation engineering approach can be extended to identify various concepts beyond refusal by simply changing the optimization targets.

ASR increases with cone dimensionality but plateaus at four dimensions, suggesting that higher-dimensional cones provide an advantage over single-direction manipulation by capturing complementary mechanisms.
refusal behaviors can be better understood via high-dimensional polyhedral cones in activation space rather than a single linear direction, highlighting their complex spatial structures.
Generally, language models differ in their refusal and response styles, which is why we use model-specific targets rather than generating them via uncensored LLMs.

domain_vocabulary.definition: A direction in a model's activation space that, when manipulated (ablated or added), controls whether the model refuses or complies with a request. Must satisfy Monotonic Scaling and Surgical Ablation properties.
A multi-dimensional polyhedral cone R_N = {Σ λ_i b_i | λ_i ≥ 0} \ {0} in activation space where all directions mediate the same concept. Discovered via Refusal Cone Optimization (RCO).

Two directions are representationally independent if ablating one has no effect on the cosine similarity between the other and model activations across a set of layers. Stricter than orthogonality; accounts for non-linear cross-layer effects.
Gradient-based algorithm finding refusal directions by optimizing ablation loss + addition loss + retain loss via gradient descent on a unit-norm direction vector.
Extension of RDO that optimizes an orthonormal basis spanning a polyhedral cone where all directions satisfy refusal properties. Uses Monte Carlo sampling and Gram-Schmidt projection.
Baseline method extracting a refusal direction by computing the difference in mean residual stream activations between harmful and harmless prompt datasets (Arditi et al., 2024).
Projecting the residual stream onto the orthogonal complement of a direction: x̃ = x - r̂r̂ᵀx. Removes the direction's influence. Applied across all layers and token positions.
Subtracting a scaled direction from residual stream activations: x̌ = x - α·r. Applied at a single layer to suppress a concept.
Adding a scaled direction to residual stream activations: x̌ = x + α·r. Applied at a single layer to induce a concept (e.g., making harmless prompts trigger refusal).
Refusal property requiring that the model's refusal probability scales monotonically with the coefficient α when adding a refusal direction to activations.
Refusal property requiring that ablating the direction causes the model to answer harmful prompts while preserving normal behavior on harmless inputs.
KL-divergence loss between model outputs with and without directional ablation on harmless instructions, preventing side effects. Tunable via weight λ_ret.
Loss term penalizing changes in cosine similarity between a direction and model activations when ablating another direction. Applied over first 90% of layers. Weight λ_ind = 200.
The cumulative representation at each transformer layer, computed as the running sum of initial embedding and all layer outputs. The space in which refusal directions and concept cones are defined.
Methods that analyze and manipulate model behavior by extracting and intervening on concept representations in the model's internal activation states (Zou et al., 2023a).
The hypothesis that concepts are encoded as linear directions in a model's residual stream, supported by the effectiveness of representation engineering methods.
The percentage of harmful prompts for which a jailbreak attack successfully elicits a harmful response, evaluated using a judge model (STRONGREJECT).

A discrete optimization method (Zou et al., 2023b) for crafting adversarial suffixes appended to prompts to bypass safety alignment. Used here to test whether RepInd directions are accessible from input space.
Token sequence appended to a prompt, optimized to circumvent safety alignment, e.g., by ablating a specific refusal direction from input space.
Using extracted directions with white-box access to manipulate model behavior, e.g., suppressing refusal to jailbreak the model.
Concepts represented by distinct activation patterns in a model's internal states, extractable via interpretability methods.
Subnetworks within a model that implement a specific function or behavior (e.g., backup circuits, information mover circuits).
Procedure for projecting a set of vectors back onto an orthonormal basis after gradient updates, used in cone optimization to maintain basis orthogonality.

Tracking cos(r, x) between a direction and model activations across layers to measure how much a direction is represented in the model's internal state—the empirical basis for testing representational independence.
Procedure from Arditi et al. (2024) for selecting the most effective refusal direction from a set of candidates based on attack success and side-effect profiles. Applied to last 20 training steps.
Full access to model weights and internal activations, required for representation engineering and directional ablation/addition methods.
Library for accessing and intervening on neural network internals (Fiotto-Kaufman et al., 2024). Implementation framework for all algorithms in this paper.
Benchmark for evaluating jailbreak attacks on LLMs (Chao et al., 2024). Used for ASR evaluation.
Fine-tuned judge model for evaluating jailbreak attack success (Souly et al., 2024). Used as the ASR evaluator.
Comprehensive collection of adversarial prompts from diverse sources (Li et al., 2024a). Source of harmful instructions for training (1,184 train, 128 validation).
Benchmark measuring how well models avoid generating false statements (Lin et al., 2021). Used to measure side effects of refusal direction interventions.
A geometric structure defined by non-negative linear combinations of basis vectors. In this context, a region in activation space where all directions share the same functional property (refusal).
The percentage of layers (e.g., first 90%) over which the RepInd loss is computed. Prevents over-constraining the model near the unembedding layer.
Sampling N directions (from a cone or via temperature) and selecting the most effective one per prompt. Used to demonstrate that cone directions capture complementary refusal aspects.
